content.assessment: Reciprocal contracts presuppose a municipal bargaining actor with fiscal and legal standing that the centralized network structure has removed; from a network-planning perspective, the mechanism's feasibility depends entirely on whether a state-initiated substitute for municipal capacity can be constructed within existing administrative geography.
opportunities: Could be structured to allocate transport or capacity investment differentially to settlements identified via population and settlement-type data (village vs town vs capital shares)
strengths: Explicitly recognizes that centralized maintenance removed the municipal capacity the model presumes, showing awareness of the actual network governance structure
Ties contract logic to co-financing and equipment grants, which could address capacity constraints in specific facilities if implemented
threats: Absent legal standing and fiscal capacity parallel to French communes, municipalities may be unable to hold up their side of reciprocal commitments, risking asymmetric non-delivery of network benefits
Inter-municipal competition over pupil 'pull' documented in Hungarian micro-regions could distort which municipalities are offered or accept such contracts, undermining uniform network coverage
weaknesses: No demonstrated network mechanism for reconciling contract-based flexibility with rigid national class-size and per-pupil funding formulas that apply uniformly regardless of local geography
Contracts do not on their own resolve geographic access or transport gaps documented in North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western rural regions
